selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

My background, shaped by a life of practical work and close-knit family values, makes me cautious about anything that seems too good to be true. I remember when I saw a post claiming that a certain herbal tea could cure diabetes—something I’d never heard of in my years of helping people with health issues through my work. That kind of claim made me feel uneasy, not because I’m skeptical by nature, but because I care deeply about truth and safety, especially for my children and aging parents. My high neuroticism means I react strongly to uncertainty, and fake content often triggers that. To manage it, I now limit my scrolling to just one hour, and I pause before sharing anything I see. I check if the source is credible, if it matches what I’ve learned from doctors or trusted elders. I also talk to my husband and daughter about what we see—this grounding in real-life conversation helps me feel more in control. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a woman in my 50s who works part-time as a plumber in Bologna, I’ve come to realize that social media, though a small part of my daily routine, can stir up anxiety—especially when I see false information about health, religion, or family. My background, shaped by a life of practical work and close-knit family values, makes me cautious about anything that seems too good to be true. I remember when I saw a post claiming that a certain herbal tea could cure diabetes—something I’d never heard of in my years of helping people with health issues through my work. That kind of claim made me feel uneasy, not because I’m skeptical by nature, but because I care deeply about truth and safety, especially for my children and aging parents. My high neuroticism means I react strongly to uncertainty, and fake content often triggers that. To manage it, I now limit my scrolling to just one hour, and I pause before sharing anything I see. I check if the source is credible, if it matches what I’ve learned from doctors or trusted elders. I also talk to my husband and daughter about what we see—this grounding in real-life conversation helps me feel more in control. In Islam, we are taught to seek knowledge and avoid deception, so I try to live by that principle. I don’t just ignore fake content—I question it, and that helps reduce the anxiety it brings. It’s not about rejecting technology, but about using it with care and wisdom.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona’s high neuroticism and cautious nature make her particularly vulnerable to anxiety from misinformation. Her background in practical work and family values supports a grounded, cautious approach. Her limited formal education and reliance on lived experience mean she trusts sources that are personal, observable, and rooted in tradition. The response integrates her religious beliefs, personal habits, and emotional sensitivity to create a realistic, empathetic strategy for managing fake content.
citations: World Health Organization (2023) on misinformation and mental health
